title: Malasadas
subtitle: Portuguese fried doughnuts rolled in sugar
excerpt: Malasadas are pillow-soft fried doughnuts from the Azores, made from a yeast dough enriched with eggs and butter. They have no hole and no filling: just a light, slightly eggy crumb rolled in sugar while still hot. The tradition crossed to Hawaii with Portuguese immigrant workers and is now equally associated with both places.
sourceNotes: Malasadas originate from the Azores and are traditionally eaten on Shrove Tuesday (Fat Tuesday) to use up sugar, butter, and eggs before Lent. Portuguese immigrants brought them to Hawaii in the late nineteenth century, where they became a fixture of Leonard's Bakery from the mid-twentieth century. The recipe follows the established Azorean and Hawaiian-Portuguese domestic tradition: no hole, no filling, fried and rolled in granulated sugar.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can be made the evening before and left to prove slowly in the fridge overnight. The next day, shape, prove for 30 minutes at room temperature, and fry.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: deep-fried at 175 degrees C; maintain the temperature throughout for even cooking

glossaryTerms.definition: Malasada dough is softer than most bread doughs and sticks to the hands. Shaping directly into balls by hand is difficult. Instead, use two spoons or a small ice cream scoop to portion the proved dough into rough rounds and drop them gently into the hot oil. They will puff and round out as they cook. The irregular shape is part of the character.

content.text: Malasadas are best eaten hot, within minutes of coming out of the oil. The
is the one step that trips people up. Do not try to hand-roll them into neat spheres. Two spoons and a confident drop into the oil gives a better result.

Combine 500 g flour, 7 g yeast, 60 g caster sugar, and 6 g salt in a stand mixer with the dough hook. Add 3 eggs, 150 ml warm milk, and 1 tsp vanilla. Mix on low for 2 minutes, then medium for 5 minutes. Add 60 g softened butter and continue mixing for another 5 minutes until the dough is smooth and elastic but quite soft. Cover and prove at room temperature for 1 hour until doubled.

Heat 1 litre vegetable oil to 175 degrees C in a deep fryer or large heavy saucepan. Knock back the dough. Using two dessertspoons or a small ice cream scoop, portion the dough into rough balls of about 45 to 50 g. Drop 4 to 5 at a time into the hot oil. Fry for 3 to 4 minutes, turning halfway, until uniformly deep golden. The centre should reach 90 degrees C on an instant-read thermometer. Remove with a spider strainer. Drain briefly on a tray, then roll immediately in 150 g granulated sugar to coat. Eat hot.

items.problem: Malasadas are golden outside but raw inside
items.solution: The oil was too hot. At above 180 degrees C, the outside sets before the inside cooks through. Keep the oil at 175 degrees C and fry for the full 4 minutes.

items.problem: Dough is too sticky to handle at all
items.solution: The dough is supposed to be soft, but if it will not hold its shape at all, it may have over-proved. Work quickly from the fridge or shape after the initial rise rather than a second prove. Lightly oil your hands instead of flouring them.
